.nr-container .nr-ol { counter-reset: nr-counter; list-style: none; padding-left: 0; margin-left: 5px; } .nr-container .nr-ol > li { position: relative; padding-left: 35px; margin-bottom: 15px; } .nr-container .nr-ol > li::before { content: counter(nr-counter) "."; counter-increment: nr-counter; position: absolute; left: 0; font-weight: 700; font-size: 18px; } .nr-container .nr-ul { .nr-container .nr-table-purple tr:nth-child(odd) { background-color: #f3f3f3; } .nr-container .nr-table-purple tr:nth-child(even) { background-color: #fff; } /* STYLE C: Procedure Table */ .nr-container .nr-table-proc th { background-color: #e6b8af !important; color: #000; } .nr-container .nr-table-proc td:nth-child(1) { background-color: #fff2cc !important; width: 30%; font-weight: .single-research_paper .research-meta h3, .single-research_paper .research-abstract h3 { border-bottom: 2px solid #e1bee7; padding-bottom: 8px; margin-top: 0; margin-bottom: 20px; color: #4a148c; } .single-research_paper .research-meta p { margin-bottom: 15px; line-height: 1.5; } /* --- The Request Button --- */ .request-btn { background: linear-gradient(45deg, #4a148c, #8e24aa); /* Purple ez-toc-heading-level-2'><a class="ez-toc-link ez-toc-heading-5" href="#SECTION_C_Long_Essay_Questions_60_Marks" >SECTION C: Long Essay Questions (60 Marks)</a></li></ul></li></ul></nav></div> <h1><span class="ez-toc-section" id="DNE_111_Foundations_of_Nursing_III_-_Dec_2022"></span>DNE 111: Foundations of Nursing III - Dec 2022<span class="ez-toc-section-end"></span></h1><div class="section"><h2><span class="ez-toc-section" id="SECTION_A_Objective_Questions_20_marks"></span>SECTION A: Objective Questions (20 marks)<span class="ez-toc-section-end"></span></h2><div class="sticker-note"> Remember to read each question carefully! The <span class="emphasis">NOT</span> questions require you to identify the FALSE statement. Take your time and eliminate wrong answers first. </div><div class="mnemonic"> For differential diagnosis questions, use the "SNAP" method: Symptoms, Nature, Associated features, Pattern </div><div class="question"><span class="question-number">1</span><div class="question-text"> The nurse should recognise that the patient's tracheostomy is blocked when there is </div><div class="options"><div class="option"><span class="option-letter">a)</span> abnormal sound from the patient's trachea.</div><div class="option"><span class="option-letter">b)</span> no air felt by the patient through tracheostomy tube.</div><div class="option"><span class="option-letter">c)</span> desaturation on the oxygen saturation monitor.</div><div class="option"><span class="option-letter">d)</span> inability to pass the suction catheter to the correct depth.</div></div><div class="correct-answer">(d) inability to pass the suction catheter to the correct depth.</div><div class="answer-explanation"> While all options can be signs of respiratory distress, the <strong>most definitive sign</strong> that a tracheostomy tube is <span class="emphasis">blocked</span> (e.g., by thick secretions, mucus plug, or kinking) is the inability to pass a suction catheter. If the catheter meets resistance and cannot be advanced through the tube, it strongly suggests an obstruction within the lumen of the tracheostomy tube itself. </div><div class="incorrect-options"><strong>(a) Abnormal sound:</strong> While gurgling or stridor can indicate partial obstruction, they don't specifically confirm a <span class="emphasis">complete blockage</span> as directly as failing to pass a suction catheter.<br><strong>(b) No air felt:</strong> This is subjective and might occur with other respiratory problems. Inability to pass a catheter is a more <span class="emphasis">objective sign</span> of tube blockage.<br><strong>(c) Desaturation:</strong> A serious sign of inadequate oxygenation, but can be caused by many other problems (e.g., pneumonia, dislodged tube). It indicates a problem but not necessarily a blocked tube as the specific cause. </div><div class="sticker-note"> 💡 <strong>Pro Tip:</strong> In airway emergencies, look for the most objective sign. Subjective symptoms can be misleading, but mechanical obstruction is definitively identified by inability to pass a catheter. </div></div><div class="question"><span class="question-number">2</span><div class="question-text"> When should nurses perform suction of the tracheostomy? </div><div class="options"><div class="option"><span class="option-letter">a)</span> As clinically indicated.</div><div class="option"><span class="option-letter">b)</span> When secretions are visible only.</div><div class="option"><span class="option-letter">c)</span> Every 24 hours.</div><div class="option"><span class="option-letter">d)</span> Every 4 hours.</div></div><div class="correct-answer">(a) As clinically indicated.</div><div class="answer-explanation"> Tracheostomy suctioning should be performed <strong>as clinically indicated</strong>, not on a fixed routine schedule. Unnecessary suctioning can cause trauma to the tracheal mucosa, hypoxia, bronchospasm, infection, and patient discomfort. Clinical indications include: audible secretions, signs of respiratory distress, desaturation, increased peak inspiratory pressures on ventilator, or inability of the patient to clear secretions effectively. </div><div class="incorrect-options"><strong>(b) When secretions are visible only:</strong> Suctioning might also be needed based on auscultation (e.g., coarse crackles) or other signs of respiratory distress even if secretions are not immediately visible.<br><strong>(c) Every 24 hours:</strong> This is far too infrequent and not based on patient need. A patient may require suctioning multiple times within a few hours.<br><strong>(d) Every 4 hours:</strong> Routine scheduled suctioning is generally not recommended unless specifically ordered. The standard of care is <span class="emphasis">assessment-based suctioning</span>. </div><div class="mnemonic"><strong>SUCTION INDICATIONS:</strong> "SAD PA" - Secretions audible, Acute distress, Desaturation, Peak pressures increased, Aspiration risk </div></div><div class="question"><span class="question-number">3</span><div class="question-text"> While assessing a patient on traction, the nurse should intervene immediately when the </div><div class="options"><div class="option"><span class="option-letter">a)</span> patient's extremities change to blue colour and have no sensations.</div><div class="option"><span class="option-letter">b)</span> pin punctures are dry.</div><div class="option"><span class="option-letter">c)</span> cords and pulleys are free and smooth.</div><div class="option"><span class="option-letter">d)</span> heights are freely hanging.</div></div><div class="correct-answer">(a) patient's extremities change to blue colour and have no sensations.</div><div class="answer-explanation"> If the patient's extremities (distal to the traction) change to a <span class="emphasis">blue color (cyanosis) and have no sensations (numbness, paresthesia)</span>, this is a critical finding indicating severe neurovascular compromise. Cyanosis suggests impaired circulation and oxygenation, and loss of sensation indicates nerve compression or damage. This is an emergency requiring immediate nursing intervention to prevent permanent tissue damage or loss of limb function. </div><div class="incorrect-options"><strong>(b) Pin punctures are dry:</strong> This is generally a <span class="emphasis">positive finding</span>, indicating no signs of infection like purulent drainage. This would not require immediate intervention.<br><strong>(c) Cords and pulleys are free and smooth:</strong> This is a <span class="emphasis">desired state</span> for traction to be effective. This is a good finding, not a reason for intervention.<br><strong>(d) Heights are freely hanging:</strong> This is a typo (likely "weights"). Freely hanging weights are <span class="emphasis">essential</span> for traction to work. This is a correct setup. </div><div class="sticker-note"> ⚠️ <strong>NEUROVASCULAR EMERGENCY:</strong> Always prioritize assessment of circulation and sensation. Blue color + no sensation = immediate action required! </div></div><div class="question"><span class="question-number">4</span><div class="question-text"> Which of the following actions should the nurse take to facilitate cast drying, in a patient who has just had a P.O.P? </div><div class="options"><div class="option"><span class="option-letter">a)</span> Cover the cast with blankets to provide extra warmth.</div><div class="option"><span class="option-letter">b)</span> Turn the patient every 2 hours.</div><div class="option"><span class="option-letter">c)</span> Increase the room temperature.</div><div class="option"><span class="option-letter">d)</span> Apply a heating pad.</div></div><div class="correct-answer">(b) Turn the patient every 2 hours.</div><div class="answer-explanation"> A fresh Plaster of Paris (P.O.P) cast takes time to dry completely (typically 24-72 hours). <strong>Turning the patient every 2 hours</strong> helps expose all parts of the cast to circulating air, promoting uniform drying and preventing pressure on any single area of the wet cast or underlying skin. This prevents indentations or flat spots that could cause pressure sores. </div><div class="incorrect-options"><strong>(a) Cover with blankets:</strong> This will <span class="emphasis">trap moisture and heat</span>, hindering the drying process and potentially leading to skin maceration under the cast.<br><strong>(c) Increase room temperature:</strong> While moderate warmth might aid evaporation, <span class="emphasis">air circulation is more critical</span>. Extreme heat should be avoided.<br><strong>(d) Apply heating pad:</strong> This is <span class="emphasis">dangerous</span> - it can cause the cast to dry too quickly on the outside while remaining wet inside, weakening the cast structure, and can cause thermal injury (burns) to the skin underneath. </div><div class="sticker-note"> 🔥 <strong>NEVER use direct heat!</strong> Cast drying requires good air circulation, not heat. Direct heat can cause burns and weaken the cast. </div></div><div class="question"><span class="question-number">5</span><div class="question-text"> Which of the following nursing interventions is appropriate to properly care for a patient with external fixation pins? </div><div class="options"><div class="option"><span class="option-letter">a)</span> Do not touch the pins.</div><div class="option"><span class="option-letter">b)</span> Loosen the screws holding the pins during cleaning.</div><div class="option"><span class="option-letter">c)</span> Follow hospital protocol for pin care.</div><div class="option"><span class="option-letter">d)</span> Cleanse with hydrogen peroxide liquid.</div></div><div class="correct-answer">(c) Follow hospital protocol for pin care.</div><div class="answer-explanation"> The most appropriate nursing intervention is to <strong>follow the specific hospital protocol or physician's orders for pin site care</strong>. Pin care protocols can vary between institutions and surgeons regarding the type of cleansing solution, frequency of care, and type of dressing. Adhering to the established protocol ensures consistency, evidence-based practice, and minimizes the risk of pin site infection, which is a significant concern with external fixation. </div><div class="incorrect-options"><strong>(a) Do not touch the pins:</strong> This is incorrect. Pin sites require <span class="emphasis">regular assessment and cleaning</span> to prevent infection. While unnecessary manipulation should be avoided, direct care is needed.<br><strong>(b) Loosen the screws:</strong> This is <span class="emphasis">absolutely incorrect and dangerous</span>. The screws maintain bone alignment and stability. Loosening them could compromise fracture reduction and stability, leading to malunion or nonunion.<br><strong>(d) Cleanse with hydrogen peroxide:</strong> This is controversial and often not recommended. Hydrogen peroxide can be <span class="emphasis">cytotoxic (damaging to healthy cells)</span>, potentially impairing wound healing and irritating the skin. </div><div class="mnemonic"><strong>PIN CARE:</strong> "PROTOCOL" - Pin site assessment, Regular observation, Oral antibiotics if needed, Timely cleaning, Orofacial protection, Clean technique, Observation for infection, Long-term monitoring </div></div><div class="question"><span class="question-number">6</span><div class="question-text"> If the nurse does NOT put a patient for lumbar puncture in a side-lying position with the back close to the edge of the bed, then the nurse should make the patient to </div><div class="options"><div class="option"><span class="option-letter">a)</span> sit with the back perpendicular to the edge of the bed leaning over a bedside table.</div><div class="option"><span class="option-letter">b)</span> stand straight leaning over the wall.</div><div class="option"><span class="option-letter">c)</span> sit with the back straight supported with pillows.</div><div class="option"><span class="option-letter">d)</span> bend the back towards the edge of the bed.</div></div><div class="correct-answer">(a) sit with the back perpendicular to the edge of the bed leaning over a bedside table.</div><div class="answer-explanation"> A lumbar puncture requires the patient's lumbar spine to be <strong>flexed to widen the interspinous spaces</strong>, allowing easier access for the needle. If the side-lying fetal position is not used, the alternative standard position is the <strong>sitting position</strong>. The patient sits on the edge of the bed, with feet supported, and leans forward, often resting their arms and head on a padded overbed table. This forward flexion helps to open up the lumbar vertebral spaces. </div><div class="incorrect-options"><strong>(b) Stand straight leaning over the wall:</strong> This would not provide adequate lumbar flexion or stability for the procedure and is not a standard position.<br><strong>(c) Sit with the back straight:</strong> Sitting with the back straight does not achieve the <span class="emphasis">necessary lumbar flexion</span> to open the intervertebral spaces. Flexion (curving the lower back outwards) is key.<br><strong>(d) Bend the back towards the edge:</strong> This is vague and doesn't fully describe the optimal supported, flexed sitting posture leaning forward. Option (a) is more precise and complete. </div><div class="sticker-note"> 🪑 <strong>Lumbar Puncture Positions:</strong> Side-lying fetal position OR sitting upright and leaning forward. Both achieve lumbar flexion to widen interspinous spaces. </div></div><div class="question"><span class="question-number">7</span><div class="question-text"> After a lumba puncture procedure is completed, the nurse should instruct the patient to </div><div class="options"><div class="option"><span class="option-letter">a)</span> flex the knees up to the chest.</div><div class="option"><span class="option-letter">b)</span> keep the head raised.</div><div class="option"><span class="option-letter">c)</span> remain on bed rest with the head of bed flat.</div><div class="option"><span class="option-letter">d)</span> reduce oral intake of fluids.</div></div><div class="correct-answer">(c) remain on bed rest with the head of bed flat.</div><div class="answer-explanation"> After a lumbar puncture, a common instruction to help prevent or minimize a <strong>post-lumbar puncture headache (PLPH)</strong> is for the patient to remain on bed rest with the head of the bed flat (supine position) for a specified period. Lying flat is believed to reduce CSF pressure at the puncture site and allow the dural hole to seal more effectively. While the evidence is debated, it remains a common instruction. </div><div class="incorrect-options"><strong>(a) Flex the knees up to chest:</strong> This position (fetal position) is used <span class="emphasis">during</span> the lumbar puncture to open the spinal spaces. It is not the recommended position <span class="emphasis">after</span> the procedure.<br><strong>(b) Keep the head raised:</strong> Keeping the head raised (sitting up) immediately after a lumbar puncture is generally discouraged as it might <span class="emphasis">increase CSF leakage</span> and the risk or severity of PLPH.<br><strong>(d) Reduce oral intake of fluids:</strong> On the contrary, patients are usually encouraged to <span class="emphasis">increase their oral fluid intake</span> (unless contraindicated) to help replenish CSF volume and may help reduce PLPH. </div><div class="mnemonic"><strong>POST-LP CARE:</strong> "FLAT" - Fluids increased, Lie flat, Assess for headache, Track vital signs </div></div><div class="question"><span class="question-number">8</span><div class="question-text"> Which of thefollowing nursing diagnoses is appropriate for a patient who has undergone colostomy? </div><div class="options"><div class="option"><span class="option-letter">a)</span> Hyperthermia related to infected wound.</div><div class="option"><span class="option-letter">b)</span> Ineffective breathing pattern related to congestion in the stomach.</div><div class="option"><span class="option-letter">c)</span> Imbalanced nutrition less than body requirements.</div><div class="option"><span class="option-letter">d)</span> Disturbedbody image related to new ostomy.</div></div><div class="correct-answer">(d) Disturbedbody image related to new ostomy.</div><div class="answer-explanation"> A colostomy involves surgically creating an opening (stoma) on the abdomen through which feces are eliminated into an external pouch. This results in a significant alteration to the body's appearance and normal eliminatory function. Many patients experience <strong>Disturbed Body Image related to the new ostomy</strong>. This nursing diagnosis addresses the negative feelings, perceptions, and cognitive disruption a person may have about their physical self, including concerns about appearance, odor, social acceptance, sexuality, and overall self-concept. </div><div class="incorrect-options"><strong>(a) Hyperthermia related to infected wound:</strong> While wound infection is a potential complication, "Disturbed Body Image" is a more <span class="emphasis">universally applicable and immediate psychosocial diagnosis</span> related directly to the presence of the ostomy itself.<br><strong>(b) Ineffective breathing pattern related to congestion in the stomach:</strong> "Congestion in the stomach" is not a standard medical term that would directly cause an ineffective breathing pattern. This is not specifically or typically related to having a colostomy.<br><strong>(c) Imbalanced nutrition less than body requirements:</strong> While nutritional issues can arise, they are not as directly and universally linked to the <span class="emphasis">fact of having a colostomy</span> as disturbed body image is. </div><div class="sticker-note"> 💔 <strong>Psychosocial Impact:</strong> Colostomy significantly affects body image and self-esteem. Addressing this is crucial for patient's overall well-being and adaptation. </div></div><div class="question"><span class="question-number">9</span><div class="question-text"> Which of the following should NOT be included in the nurse's teaching for a patient with eye inflammation? </div><div class="options"><div class="option"><span class="option-letter">a)</span> Good eye hygiene.</div><div class="option"><span class="option-letter">b)</span> How to prevent spread of infection.</div><div class="option"><span class="option-letter">c)</span> How to wear contact lenses.</div><div class="option"><span class="option-letter">d)</span> Administration of ointments or drops.</div></div><div class="correct-answer">(c) How to wear contact lenses.</div><div class="answer-explanation"> When a patient has eye inflammation (e.g., conjunctivitis, keratitis, uveitis), <strong>wearing contact lenses is generally contraindicated</strong> and can worsen the condition, delay healing, or increase the risk of complications (like corneal ulcers). Therefore, teaching a patient how to wear contact lenses during an active episode of eye inflammation would be inappropriate and potentially harmful. The patient should be advised to <span class="emphasis">avoid wearing contact lenses</span> until the inflammation has completely resolved. </div><div class="incorrect-options"><strong>(a) Good eye hygiene:</strong> This is <span class="emphasis">essential teaching</span> - includes handwashing, avoiding touching/rubbing eyes, using clean tissues.<br><strong>(b) Prevent spread of infection:</strong> If inflammation is infectious, teaching measures to prevent spread to the other eye or other people is <span class="emphasis">crucial</span>.<br><strong>(d) Administration of ointments or drops:</strong> If prescribed, the nurse must teach the correct technique for instilling eye medications to ensure efficacy and prevent contamination. </div><div class="mnemonic"><strong>EYE INFLAMMATION TEACHING:</strong> "HAPI" - Hygiene, Avoid contacts, Prevent spread, Instill drops correctly </div></div><div class="question"><span class="question-number">10</span><div class="question-text"> After applying ointment or drops in the patient's eye, the nurse asks the patient to close the eye and places a disposable gauze over the eye socket in a procedure referred to as eye </div><div class="options"><div class="option"><span class="option-letter">a)</span> dressing.</div><div class="option"><span class="option-letter">b)</span> patching.</div><div class="option"><span class="option-letter">c)</span> covering.</div><div class="option"><span class="option-letter">d)</span> protection.</div></div><div class="correct-answer">(b) patching.</div><div class="answer-explanation"> The procedure described – applying medication, having the patient close their eye, and then placing a disposable gauze (often secured with tape) over the eye socket – is most accurately referred to as eye <strong>patching</strong>. Eye patching is done for various reasons, such as to protect an injured or infected eye, promote healing after surgery, reduce eye movement, prevent rubbing, or manage conditions like corneal abrasion or diplopia (double vision). </div><div class="incorrect-options"><strong>(a) Dressing:</strong> While a patch is a type of dressing, "patching" is the <span class="emphasis">more specific term</span> for covering the eye in this manner.<br><strong>(c) Covering:</strong> This is a very general term and less specific than "patching" in a clinical context.<br><strong>(d) Protection:</strong> This describes the <span class="emphasis">purpose</span> of the patch rather than the name of the procedure itself. </div><div class="sticker-note"> 👁️‍🗨️ <strong>Eye Patching:</strong> Occludes the eye, provides rest, protection, and promotes healing. Different from a dressing which covers a wound directly. </div></div><div class="question"><span class="question-number">11</span><div class="question-text"> Insertion of a tracheostomy tube is indicated to </div><div class="options"><div class="option"><span class="option-letter">a)</span> administer drugs.</div><div class="option"><span class="option-letter">b)</span> soften the trachea.</div><div class="option"><span class="option-letter">c)</span> reduce dead air apace and foreign body in airway.</div><div class="option"><span class="option-letter">d)</span> promote hyperventilation.</div></div><div class="correct-answer">(c) reduce dead air space and foreign body in airway.</div><div class="answer-explanation"> A tracheostomy is indicated to: <strong>bypass upper airway obstruction</strong>, facilitate prolonged mechanical ventilation, aid tracheobronchial toilet (secretion removal), and protect the airway. Option (c) touches on some benefits: it does reduce anatomical dead space compared to breathing through the upper airway, and can bypass a foreign body if it causes persistent upper airway obstruction. While the primary indications are usually stated more broadly, (c) is the closest fit among the given choices. </div><div class="incorrect-options"><strong>(a) Administer drugs:</strong> While some emergency drugs can be instilled via tracheostomy, this is NOT a primary indication for *inserting* a tracheostomy tube.<br><strong>(b) Soften the trachea:</strong> A tracheostomy tube <span class="emphasis">does not soften</span> the trachea. Long-term presence can sometimes lead to tracheomalacia.<br><strong>(d) Promote hyperventilation:</strong> Hyperventilation is not a therapeutic goal promoted by tracheostomy insertion. A tracheostomy facilitates effective ventilation, but doesn't inherently promote hyperventilation. </div><div class="mnemonic"><strong>TRACHEOSTOMY INDICATIONS:</strong> "BATS" - Bypass obstruction, Airway protection, Tracheobronchial toilet, Support prolonged ventilation </div></div><div class="question"><span class="question-number">12</span><div class="question-text"> During abdominal paracentesis, the nurse should </div><div class="options"><div class="option"><span class="option-letter">a)</span> hold the drainage tube and inflate it.</div><div class="option"><span class="option-letter">b)</span> place the patient in a sitting up position.</div><div class="option"><span class="option-letter">c)</span> keep the patient on Nil by mouth.</div><div class="option"><span class="option-letter">d)</span> support the abdomen with gauze.</div></div><div class="correct-answer">(b) place the patient in a sitting up position.</div><div class="answer-explanation"> During abdominal paracentesis, the nurse should position the patient <strong>sitting upright in bed (High Fowler's) or on the side of the bed leaning over an overbed table</strong>, with feet supported. This position allows ascitic fluid to pool in the lower abdomen by gravity, facilitating easier needle insertion and fluid drainage. It also allows the bowel to float posteriorly, away from the anterior puncture site, reducing the risk of perforation. </div><div class="incorrect-options"><strong>(a) Hold drainage tube and inflate it:</strong> Drainage tubes used in paracentesis are typically for <span class="emphasis">passive drainage</span>; they do not usually have an inflatable component.<br><strong>(c) Keep patient Nil by mouth:</strong> NBM is not usually a routine requirement for a standard abdominal paracentesis performed under local anesthesia.<br><strong>(d) Support abdomen with gauze:</strong> While a dressing will be applied to the puncture site <span class="emphasis">after</span> the procedure, supporting the abdomen during the procedure is not a primary nursing responsibility related to the core technique. </div><div class="sticker-note"> 🪑 <strong>Paracentesis Positioning:</strong> Upright position uses gravity to pool fluid in dependent areas, making it easier to access and drain from lower abdomen. </div></div><div class="question"><span class="question-number">13</span><div class="question-text"> Which of the following instructions should nurses give to a patient prior to an abdominal paracentesis? </div><div class="options"><div class="option"><span class="option-letter">a)</span> strict bed rest after the procedure.</div><div class="option"><span class="option-letter">b)</span> empty the bowel before the procedure.</div><div class="option"><span class="option-letter">c)</span> empty the bladder before the procedure.</div><div class="option"><span class="option-letter">d)</span> maintain nil by mouth.</div></div><div class="correct-answer">(c) empty the bladder before the procedure.</div><div class="answer-explanation"> One of the most important instructions prior to abdominal paracentesis is to <strong>empty their bladder (void) completely</strong>. The insertion site for the paracentesis needle or catheter is typically in the lower abdomen. An empty bladder reduces its size and moves it away from the typical needle insertion site, significantly minimizing the risk of accidental bladder perforation during the paracentesis. </div><div class="incorrect-options"><strong>(a) Strict bed rest after:</strong> While some observation or rest is typical, "strict bed rest" for an extended period is not always required, especially after a simple diagnostic tap.<br><strong>(b) Empty the bowel before:</strong> While having an empty bowel might be more comfortable, it is generally not a specific or routine instruction for abdominal paracentesis in the same way that emptying the bladder is for safety.<br><strong>(d) Maintain nil by mouth:</strong> As mentioned in the previous question, NBM is usually not required for a standard abdominal paracentesis performed under local anesthesia. </div><div class="mnemonic"><strong>PRE-PARACENTESIS:</strong> "VOID" - Void bladder, Obtain consent, Inform patient, Dressing ready </div></div><div class="question"><span class="question-number">14</span><div class="question-text"> Which of the following solutions should the nurse use to clean the tracheostomy tube? </div><div class="options"><div class="option"><span class="option-letter">a)</span> Normal saline.</div><div class="option"><span class="option-letter">b)</span> Hibicet.</div><div class="option"><span class="option-letter">c)</span> Alcohol.</div><div class="option"><span class="option-letter">d)</span> Sodium Bicarbonate.</div></div><div class="correct-answer">(a) Normal saline.</div><div class="answer-explanation"> When cleaning the inner cannula of a reusable tracheostomy tube, <strong>sterile normal saline (0.9% sodium chloride)</strong> is a commonly recommended and safe solution. It is isotonic and non-irritating to the tissues. It effectively helps to loosen and remove dried secretions and mucus. Some protocols may also involve using a half-strength solution of hydrogen peroxide followed by a normal saline rinse for the inner cannula, but normal saline is a staple for rinsing and general cleaning. </div><div class="incorrect-options"><strong>(b) Hibicet:</strong> This is not universally recognized. While dilute chlorhexidine solutions might be used for stoma site care, they're not typically used for cleaning the <span class="emphasis">inside</span> of the tracheostomy tube itself due to potential for irritation if aspirated.<br><strong>(c) Alcohol:</strong> Alcohol is generally <span class="emphasis">too harsh and drying</span> for cleaning tracheostomy tubes or stoma sites. It can irritate the mucosa and skin.<br><strong>(d) Sodium Bicarbonate:</strong> Sometimes used to help loosen very thick, tenacious mucus, but for routine cleaning of the tracheostomy tube, <span class="emphasis">sterile normal saline is the more standard and universally accepted</span> solution. </div><div class="sticker-note"> 💧 <strong>Normal Saline is Safe:</strong> Isotonic, non-irritating, and effective for cleaning. Always follow institutional protocol. </div></div><div class="question"><span class="question-number">15</span><div class="question-text"> A feeding tube is recommended when a patient is </div><div class="options"><div class="option"><span class="option-letter">a)</span> having difficulty with eating food.</div><div class="option"><span class="option-letter">b)</span> having sores in the mouth.</div><div class="option"><span class="option-letter">c)</span> loosing weight.</div><div class="option"><span class="option-letter">d)</span> not meeting nutritional needs orally.</div></div><div class="correct-answer">(d) not meeting nutritional needs orally.</div><div class="answer-explanation"> Enteral feeding via a feeding tube is generally recommended when a patient has a functioning gastrointestinal (GI) tract but is <strong>unable to meet their nutritional needs adequately through oral intake alone</strong>. This is the most comprehensive and encompassing reason. The other options can be contributing factors to this inability, but (d) captures the core indication: an existing or anticipated nutritional deficit that cannot be rectified by normal eating. </div><div class="incorrect-options"><strong>(a) Having difficulty with eating:</strong> This is a common reason <span class="emphasis">why</span> someone might not meet their nutritional needs orally (e.g., dysphagia). It's a cause, leading to the indication in (d).<br><strong>(b) Having sores in the mouth:</strong> This can make eating difficult and lead to inadequate oral intake, thus contributing to the situation in (d).<br><strong>(c) Losing weight:</strong> This is often a consequence of not meeting nutritional needs orally and can be a sign that a feeding tube might be necessary. It's an outcome that points towards the core issue in (d). </div><div class="mnemonic"><strong>ENTERAL FEEDING INDICATIONS:</strong> "NEEDS" - Not meeting nutritional needs, Eating difficulties, Extreme weight loss, Dysphagia, Severe mouth sores </div></div><div class="question"><span class="question-number">16</span><div class="question-text"> The hydration status of a patient on a feeding tube is monitored by </div><div class="options"><div class="option"><span class="option-letter">a)</span> input and output.</div><div class="option"><span class="option-letter">b)</span> daily weight.</div><div class="option"><span class="option-letter">c)</span> electrolyte balance.</div><div class="option"><span class="option-letter">d)</span> amount of urine passed.</div></div><div class="correct-answer">(a) input and output.</div><div class="answer-explanation"> While all the options are relevant to assessing fluid balance, monitoring <strong>input and output (I&O)</strong> is a comprehensive way to directly track hydration status in a patient receiving tube feeding. Careful I&O charting helps determine the patient's net fluid balance (whether they are retaining too much fluid or losing too much). This includes all fluids taken in (formula, water flushes, IV fluids) and all fluids lost (urine, emesis, diarrhea, drainage). </div><div class="incorrect-options"><strong>(b) Daily weight:</strong> This is a very important indicator of fluid status, but I&O provides a <span class="emphasis">more detailed breakdown</span> of fluid dynamics.<br><strong>(c) Electrolyte balance:</strong> Electrolyte levels are indicators of the <span class="emphasis">consequences or causes</span> of hydration issues, rather than a direct measure of fluid volume.<br><strong>(d) Amount of urine passed:</strong> This is a critical component of the "output" side of an I&O chart, but it's only one part of the overall fluid balance picture. </div><div class="sticker-note"> 📊 <strong>Comprehensive Assessment:</strong> Best practice involves using multiple parameters: I&O, daily weights, clinical signs (skin turgor, mucous membranes), urine specific gravity, and lab values (electrolytes, BUN, creatinine). </div></div><div class="question"><span class="question-number">17</span><div class="question-text"> Which of the following should the nurse observe on a patient who is on Gallow's traction? </div><div class="options"><div class="option"><span class="option-letter">a)</span> Cords and pulleys that are free and smoothly running.</div><div class="option"><span class="option-letter">b)</span> Bandages that are secure, unwrinkled and exerting even pressure.</div><div class="option"><span class="option-letter">c)</span> Secure and freely hanging weight.</div><div class="option"><span class="option-letter">d)</span> Stirrup not pressing on the patient's skin.</div></div><div class="correct-answer">(c) Secure and freely hanging weight.</div><div class="answer-explanation"> For any traction system, including Gallow's (Bryant's) traction, it is absolutely essential that the <strong>weights are secure (properly attached) and hanging freely</strong>, not resting on the bed, floor, or any other obstruction. If the weights are not hanging freely, the prescribed amount of traction force will not be applied to the limb, rendering the traction ineffective for its purpose (e.g., reducing a fracture, immobilizing a limb). While all options are important observations, (c) is most critical for ensuring the traction itself is functioning. </div><div class="incorrect-options"><strong>(a) Cords and pulleys:</strong> This is also a very important observation, but if the weights aren't hanging freely, the system won't work regardless of how smooth the pulleys are.<br><strong>(b) Bandages:</strong> Important for patient safety and comfort, but secondary to ensuring the mechanical traction is actually working.<br><strong>(d) Stirrup not pressing:</strong> Important for preventing skin complications, but doesn't affect whether the traction force is being applied. </div><div class="mnemonic"><strong>TRACTION EFFECTIVENESS:</strong> "WEIGHTS" - Weights hanging freely, Even pressure, Inspection of pin sites, Girth checks, Humidification, Traction maintained, Skin integrity </div></div><div class="question"><span class="question-number">18</span><div class="question-text"> For which of the following reasons should a wound be dressed? </div><div class="options"><div class="option"><span class="option-letter">a)</span> Keep the wound sterile.</div><div class="option"><span class="option-letter">b)</span> Keep the wound intact.</div><div class="option"><span class="option-letter">c)</span> Absorption of excess fluid and infection control.</div><div class="option"><span class="option-letter">d)</span> Immobilise the wound.</div></div><div class="correct-answer">(c) Absorption of excess fluid and infection control.</div><div class="answer-explanation"> One of the primary reasons for wound dressing is <strong>absorption of excess fluid (exudate)</strong> and <strong>infection control</strong>. An appropriate dressing helps absorb excess fluid, which prevents maceration (softening and breakdown) of surrounding healthy skin, reduces discomfort, and manages odor. A dressing also acts as a physical barrier to protect the wound from external contamination by microorganisms, reducing infection risk. </div><div class="incorrect-options"><strong>(a) Keep the wound sterile:</strong> While a sterile dressing is applied using aseptic technique, it is very difficult to keep an open wound truly "sterile" once it exists. The goal is more accurately to <span class="emphasis">keep it clean and prevent infection</span>.<br><strong>(b) Keep the wound intact:</strong> The wound already exists; the dressing manages it. "Keeping it intact" isn't the primary overarching reason.<br><strong>(d) Immobilise the wound:</strong> While some specialized dressings can provide support, the primary purpose of most standard wound dressings is <span class="emphasis">not immobilization</span>. Immobilization is usually achieved by other means like splints or casts. </div><div class="sticker-note"> 🩹 <strong>Dressing Purposes:</strong> Protect wound, Absorb exudate, Prevent infection, Maintain moist environment, Provide comfort </div></div><div class="question"><span class="question-number">19</span><div class="question-text"> When bandaging a limb, the nurse stands </div><div class="options"><div class="option"><span class="option-letter">a)</span> behind the patient.</div><div class="option"><span class="option-letter">b)</span> infront of the patient.</div><div class="option"><span class="option-letter">c)</span> infront of the part to be bandaged.</div><div class="option"><span class="option-letter">d)</span> opposite the part to be bandaged.</div></div><div class="correct-answer">(c) infront of the part to be bandaged.</div><div class="answer-explanation"> When applying a bandage to a limb, the nurse should generally position themselves <strong>in front of the part to be bandaged</strong>. This allows the nurse to: have a clear view of the area being bandaged, maintain good body mechanics and control, easily manipulate the bandage roll, and observe the patient's comfort and the effect of the bandage as it is being applied. </div><div class="incorrect-options"><strong>(a) Behind the patient:</strong> This would make it very difficult to see and effectively bandage a limb that is typically in front of or to the side of the patient.<br><strong>(b) In front of the patient:</strong> While generally correct, (c) is more specific. "In front of the patient" could still mean the nurse is not directly facing the specific limb segment being worked on.<br><strong>(d) Opposite the part:</strong> This is a bit ambiguous but generally implies facing the part, which is consistent with (c). However, "in front of the part" is a clearer description of the optimal working position. </div><div class="sticker-note"> 🧍‍♀️ <strong>Bandaging Position:</strong> Stand facing the body part being bandaged for best visibility, control, and patient observation. </div></div><div class="question"><span class="question-number">20</span><div class="question-text"> A pull applied to the skin and transmitted through the soft tissues to the bone is Called __________ traction. </div><div class="options"><div class="option"><span class="option-letter">a)</span> Spinal.</div><div class="option"><span class="option-letter">b)</span> Skeletal.</div><div class="option"><span class="option-letter">c)</span> Gallow's.</div><div class="option"><span class="option-letter">d)</span> Skin.</div></div><div class="correct-answer">(d) Skin.</div><div class="answer-explanation"><strong>Skin traction</strong> is a type of traction where the pulling force is applied directly to the skin and underlying soft tissues using adhesive straps, tapes, boots, or slings. The traction force is then transmitted from the skin, through the subcutaneous tissues and fascia, to the bone. It is generally used for lighter weights and shorter durations compared to skeletal traction. </div><div class="incorrect-options"><strong>(a) Spinal:</strong> This refers to traction applied to the spine (cervical or pelvic), but "spinal" describes the <span class="emphasis">location</span>, not the method of force application.<br><strong>(b) Skeletal:</strong> Skeletal traction involves applying the pulling force <span class="emphasis">directly to the bone</span> itself via surgically inserted pins, wires, or tongs.<br><strong>(c) Gallow's:</strong> Gallow's traction (Bryant's traction) is a <span class="emphasis">type of skin traction</span> used for young children with femur fractures. </div><div class="mnemonic"><strong>TRACTION TYPES:</strong> "SKIN" - Skin (applied to skin), Skeletal (applied to bone), Spinal (applied to spine) </div></div></div><div class="section"><h2><span class="ez-toc-section" id="Fill_in_the_Blank_Spaces_10_marks"></span>Fill in the Blank Spaces (10 marks)<span class="ez-toc-section-end"></span></h2><div class="question"><span class="question-number">21</span><div class="question-text"> Feeding the patient by means of an opening directly into the stomach through the abdominal wall is termed a __________. </div><div class="fill-blank"><div class="answer-box">Gastrostomy (feeding)</div><div class="answer-explanation"> A <strong>gastrostomy</strong> is a surgical procedure to create an artificial opening (stoma) from the abdominal wall directly into the stomach. A tube (gastrostomy tube or G-tube) is then inserted through this opening to allow for direct enteral feeding when a patient cannot take adequate nutrition orally. This method of feeding is referred to as gastrostomy feeding. </div></div></div><div class="question"><span class="question-number">22</span><div class="question-text"> Leakage of Cerebral Spinal fluid through the dural defect following needle withdrawal is a complication of __________. </div><div class="fill-blank"><div class="answer-box">Lumbar puncture (or spinal tap / dural puncture)</div><div class="answer-explanation"> Leakage of cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) through the puncture site in the dura mater (the tough outer membrane surrounding the spinal cord and brain) after the needle is withdrawn is a known potential complication of a <strong>lumbar puncture</strong> (also called a spinal tap). This CSF leakage can lead to a decrease in intracranial pressure, causing a post-lumbar puncture headache (PLPH), which typically worsens when upright and improves when lying flat. </div></div></div><div class="question"><span class="question-number">23</span><div class="question-text"> Burns of the neck, bulbar paralysis, severe asthmatic attack and reduction of the dead air space within the lungs are indications fora procedure known as __________. </div><div class="fill-blank"><div class="answer-box">Tracheostomy</div><div class="answer-explanation"> The conditions listed – severe burns of the neck (which can cause airway swelling and obstruction), bulbar paralysis (affecting muscles for swallowing and airway protection, leading to aspiration risk), severe asthmatic attack (if leading to prolonged respiratory failure requiring ventilation), and the need to reduce dead air space (to improve ventilation efficiency or facilitate weaning from a ventilator) – are all potential indications for a <strong>tracheostomy</strong>. A tracheostomy creates a surgical airway in the neck, bypassing the upper airway, which can be crucial in these scenarios for maintaining a patent airway, facilitating mechanical ventilation, allowing for secretion removal, and reducing anatomical dead space. </div></div></div><div class="question"><span class="question-number">24</span><div class="question-text"> The nurse should prepare a drainage bottle, local anaesthesia, iodine solution, tape measure, dressing tray, trocher and cannula rubber tubing and clip as requirements for performing __________. </div><div class="fill-blank"><div class="answer-box">Abdominal paracentesis (or thoracentesis, though abdominal paracentesis fits slightly better with "drainage bottle" and "tape measure" for girth)</div><div class="answer-explanation"> The listed equipment – drainage bottle, local anesthesia, iodine solution (for skin antisepsis), tape measure (often used to measure abdominal girth before and after fluid removal in ascites), dressing tray, trocar and cannula, rubber tubing, and clip – are all standard requirements for performing an <strong>abdominal paracentesis</strong>. This procedure involves inserting a trocar and cannula into the peritoneal cavity to drain accumulated ascitic fluid. A thoracentesis (draining pleural fluid) also uses similar equipment but a tape measure for abdominal girth wouldn't be primary for that. </div></div></div><div class="question"><span class="question-number">25</span><div class="question-text"> A pull exerted on the part of the limb against a pull of compared strength in the opposite direction is __________. </div><div class="fill-blank"><div class="answer-box">Countertraction</div><div class="answer-explanation"> In therapeutic traction, for the primary pulling force (traction) to be effective in aligning bones or reducing muscle spasm, there must be an opposing force, called <strong>countertraction</strong>. Countertraction is a pull in the opposite direction to the main traction force, which prevents the patient's body from simply being pulled along with the traction weights. It can be provided by the patient's own body weight (e.g., by elevating the foot of the bed in leg traction), by additional weights, or by the friction of the patient's body against the bed. </div></div></div><div class="question"><span class="question-number">26</span><div class="question-text"> While carrying out traction, the nurse applies strapping smoothly to avoid wrinkles because they can cause __________. </div><div class="fill-blank"><div class="answer-box">Skin breakdown (or pressure sores / skin irritation / blisters)</div><div class="answer-explanation"> When applying skin traction, it is crucial to apply the adhesive strapping or bandages smoothly, without any wrinkles or creases. Wrinkles in the strapping can create areas of uneven pressure on the skin underneath. Over time, this concentrated pressure can irritate the skin, impair circulation to that small area, and lead to <strong>skin breakdown</strong>, pressure sores, blisters, or excoriation. Smooth application ensures that the traction force is distributed as evenly as possible over the skin surface. </div></div></div><div class="question"><span class="question-number">27</span><div class="question-text"> The type of bandage used to support an injured shoulder is __________. </div><div class="fill-blank"><div class="answer-box">Sling (or triangular bandage as a sling / shoulder spica bandage for more immobilization)</div><div class="answer-explanation"> A common and effective way to support an injured shoulder (e.g., for a clavicle fracture, shoulder dislocation after reduction, sprain, or post-operatively) is by using a <strong>sling</strong>. A triangular bandage is often folded or applied to create a sling that supports the weight of the arm, immobilizes the shoulder to some extent, and reduces pain by preventing movement. For more comprehensive immobilization of the shoulder joint, a shoulder spica bandage might be used, but a sling is the most typical initial support. </div></div></div><div class="question"><span class="question-number">28</span><div class="question-text"> Materials used for wound drainage include rubber or plastic drainage tubes and __________. </div><div class="fill-blank"><div class="answer-box">Drains (e.g., Penrose drain, Jackson-Pratt drain, Hemovac drain / gauze wicks)</div><div class="answer-explanation"> Materials used for wound drainage include various types of rubber or plastic drainage tubes (which facilitate the removal of fluid like blood, pus, or serous fluid from a wound or body cavity) and other types of <strong>drains</strong> or wicking materials. Examples include: <ul><li><strong>Penrose drain:</strong> A soft, flat rubber tube that acts as a passive drain.</li><li><strong>Jackson-Pratt (JP) drain</strong> or <strong>Hemovac drain:</strong> Closed-suction drains that use gentle negative pressure to actively pull fluid out.</li><li><strong>Gauze wicks or packing strips:</strong> Sometimes inserted into wounds to help absorb drainage or keep a wound open to drain.</li></ul></div></div></div><div class="question"><span class="question-number">29</span><div class="question-text"> In which position should a nurse put a patient on underwater seal drainage? </div><div class="fill-blank"><div class="answer-box">Semi-Fowler's (or High Fowler's / sitting upright)</div><div class="answer-explanation"> A patient with an underwater seal drainage system (chest tube drainage) is typically positioned in a <strong>Semi-Fowler's (30-45 degrees head elevation) or High Fowler's (60-90 degrees head elevation) position</strong>, or sitting upright as much as tolerated. This upright positioning helps to: <ul><li>Promote optimal lung expansion and make breathing easier.</li><li>Facilitate the drainage of air (if a pneumothorax) from the apical (upper) part of the pleural space.</li><li>Facilitate the drainage of fluid (if a hemothorax or pleural effusion) from the basal (lower) part of the pleural space by gravity.</li></ul> Lying flat should generally be avoided unless specifically indicated for short periods or during transport if unavoidable. </div></div></div><div class="question"><span class="question-number">30</span><div class="question-text"> Removal of potentially harmful substances from the stomach is known as __________. </div><div class="fill-blank"><div class="answer-box">Gastric lavage (or stomach washout / gastric suction)</div><div class="answer-explanation"> The removal of potentially harmful substances (like ingested poisons, toxins, or an overdose of medication) from the stomach is known as <strong>gastric lavage</strong>, commonly referred to as a stomach washout or stomach pumping. This procedure involves inserting a tube (orogastric or nasogastric tube) into the stomach, instilling fluid (usually water or normal saline), and then aspirating or draining the stomach contents to remove the toxic substance before it is absorbed significantly into the bloodstream. Gastric suction via a nasogastric tube can also be used to remove stomach contents, though lavage specifically implies washing out. </div></div></div><div class="section"><h2><span class="ez-toc-section" id="SECTION_B_Short_Essay_Questions_10_Marks"></span>SECTION B: Short Essay Questions (10 Marks)<span class="ez-toc-section-end"></span></h2><div class="question"><span class="question-number">31</span><div class="question-text"> State five (5) specific requirements a nurse should include on a gastrostomy feeding tray. (5 marks) </div><div class="answer-section essay-answer"><p><span class="emoji">🍽️</span>A gastrostomy feeding tray should be meticulously prepared by the nurse at Nurses Revision Uganda to ensure safe and effective administration of enteral nutrition. Specific requirements to include are:</p><ol><li><strong>Prescribed Enteral Formula:</strong><span class="emoji">🍼</span><span class="sub-point">Requirement:</span> The correct type and amount of prescribed enteral feeding formula, at room temperature (or warmed slightly if indicated by policy, but never hot). Check the expiry date and integrity of the container. <span class="rationale">Rationale: Ensures the patient receives the specific nutrition ordered by the physician or dietitian, tailored to their individual needs. Administering formula at room temperature minimizes gastrointestinal upset. Verifying expiry and integrity prevents administration of spoiled or contaminated feed.</span></li><li><strong>Appropriate Feeding Syringe (Enteral Syringe):</strong><span class="emoji">💉</span><span class="sub-point">Requirement:</span> A large-tipped catheter syringe (typically 50-60 mL capacity), specifically designed for enteral feeding (often color-coded purple or labeled "Enteral Use Only" to prevent accidental connection to IV lines). <span class="rationale">Rationale: Enteral syringes have a tip that is incompatible with IV luer lock systems, preventing accidental intravenous administration of enteral formula, which can be fatal. The large volume allows for efficient administration of bolus feeds or for flushing.</span></li><li><strong>Water for Flushing:</strong><span class="emoji">💧</span><span class="sub-point">Requirement:</span> A container of clean water (sterile water for immunocompromised patients or as per hospital policy, otherwise tap water may be acceptable for stable patients at home) at room temperature, typically 30-50 mL for flushing before and after feeding, and before and after medication administration. <span class="rationale">Rationale: Flushing the gastrostomy tube before feeding ensures patency and clears any residual feed or medication. Flushing after feeding and medication administration prevents tube blockage and ensures the full dose of feed/medication is delivered. Water also contributes to the patient's hydration.</span></li><li><strong>Measuring Container/Graduate:</strong><span class="emoji">📏</span><span class="sub-point">Requirement:</span> A clean graduated measuring container if the formula needs to be decanted from a larger container or if water for flushing needs to be precisely measured. <span class="rationale">Rationale: Accurate measurement of formula and flush volumes is essential to ensure the patient receives the prescribed amount of nutrition and hydration, and to maintain accurate intake records.</span></li><li><strong>Clean Gloves and Protective Cover/Towel:</strong><span class="emoji">🧤</span><span class="sub-point">Requirement:</span> Clean, non-sterile examination gloves for the nurse to wear during the procedure, and a clean towel or disposable protective cover to place under the gastrostomy tube connection or over the patient's clothing/bedding. <span class="rationale">Rationale: Gloves maintain medical asepsis and protect the nurse. The protective cover prevents soiling of the patient's clothes or bed linens from accidental spills of formula or flush water.</span></li><li><strong>pH Indicator Strips (if checking gastric placement):</strong><span class="emoji">🧪</span><span class="sub-point">Requirement:</span> pH indicator strips if hospital policy requires checking gastric aspirate pH to confirm tube placement before initiating feeding (though for established gastrostomy tubes, this may be less frequent than for newly inserted NG tubes). <span class="rationale">Rationale: Verifying gastric placement (pH typically <5.5) helps to ensure the feed is delivered into the stomach and not into an inadvertently displaced tube, reducing aspiration risk, although visual inspection of the G-tube site and length is also key for G-tubes.</span></li><li><strong>Clamp (if not already on the G-tube extension set):</strong><span class="emoji">🔒</span><span class="sub-point">Requirement:</span> A tube clamp may be needed to clamp the gastrostomy tube or extension set during connection/disconnection of the syringe or feeding bag to prevent leakage of gastric contents or air entry. <span class="rationale">Rationale: Prevents spillage and maintains a closed system when not actively feeding or flushing.</span></li></ol></div></div><div class="question"><span class="question-number">32</span><div class="question-text"> Outline five (5) nursing interventions a nurse should implement while carrying out colostomy care. (5 marks) </div><div class="answer-section essay-answer"><p><span class="emoji">🩹</span>Providing colostomy care is a vital nursing intervention at Nurses Revision Uganda that promotes patient comfort, hygiene, skin integrity, and psychosocial well-being. Effective care involves several key steps:</p><ol><li><strong>Assess the Stoma and Peristomal Skin:</strong><span class="emoji">👀</span><span class="sub-point">Intervention:</span> Before and during the pouch change, carefully assess the stoma for color (should be moist and beefy red/pink), size, shape, and any signs of complications (e.g., necrosis, retraction, prolapse, stenosis). Inspect the peristomal skin (skin around the stoma) for redness, irritation, breakdown, rash, or signs of infection. <span class="rationale">Rationale: Regular assessment detects early signs of stoma complications or peristomal skin problems, allowing for prompt intervention and prevention of further issues. A healthy stoma and intact peristomal skin are crucial for successful ostomy management.</span></li><li><strong>Gentle Cleansing of the Stoma and Peristomal Skin:</strong><span class="emoji">🧼💧</span><span class="sub-point">Intervention:</span> Gently cleanse the stoma and the surrounding peristomal skin with warm water and a soft cloth or disposable wipe. Avoid using harsh soaps, alcohol-based solutions, or oily substances unless specifically indicated, as these can irritate the skin or interfere with pouch adherence. Pat the skin thoroughly dry. <span class="rationale">Rationale: Gentle cleansing removes any fecal matter and maintains hygiene, reducing odor and the risk of skin irritation or infection. Ensuring the skin is completely dry before applying a new pouch is essential for good adhesion and to prevent skin maceration.</span></li><li><strong>Measure the Stoma and Ensure Proper Pouch Fit:</strong><span class="emoji">📏</span><span class="sub-point">Intervention:</span> Use a stoma measuring guide to accurately measure the size and shape of the stoma, especially in the early postoperative period when it may change size. Cut the opening in the new skin barrier (wafer) of the ostomy pouch to be just slightly larger than the stoma (typically 1/16 to 1/8 inch or 2-3 mm larger) to ensure a snug fit without constricting the stoma or exposing too much peristomal skin. <span class="rationale">Rationale: A properly fitting pouching system is critical. An opening that is too small can cut or irritate the stoma. An opening that is too large will expose the peristomal skin to fecal effluent, leading to skin irritation, breakdown, and leakage. Stoma size can change, so regular measurement is important initially.</span></li><li><strong>Apply the New Pouching System Securely:</strong><span class="emoji">🩹✅</span><span class="sub-point">Intervention:</span> Apply the new skin barrier/pouch carefully, ensuring it adheres smoothly and securely to the dry peristomal skin without wrinkles, especially around the stoma. If using a two-piece system, ensure the pouch is securely attached to the skin barrier flange. Use skin barrier paste or rings if needed to fill in uneven skin surfaces and create a better seal. <span class="rationale">Rationale: A secure, leak-proof seal is essential to protect the peristomal skin from irritation by fecal output, prevent leakage and odor, and provide the patient with confidence and comfort. Wrinkles in the skin barrier can create channels for leakage.</span></li><li><strong>Provide Patient Education, Emotional Support, and Encourage Self-Care:</strong><span class="emoji">🗣️❤️</span><span class="sub-point">Intervention:</span> Use the opportunity during colostomy care to educate the patient (and/or caregiver) about stoma care techniques, signs of complications to report, diet and fluid management, odor control, and available resources. Provide emotional support, encourage verbalization of feelings about the ostomy, and actively involve the patient in their care as much as possible to promote independence and positive body image. <span class="rationale">Rationale: Living with a colostomy requires significant adjustment. Education empowers the patient to manage their ostomy effectively. Emotional support helps them cope with changes in body image and lifestyle. Promoting self-care fosters independence, control, and adaptation.</span></li><li><strong>Appropriate Emptying and Disposal of the Old Pouch:</strong><span class="emoji">🗑️</span><span class="sub-point">Intervention:</span> Before removing the old pouch, empty its contents into a toilet or designated receptacle if it's a drainable pouch. Dispose of the used pouch and supplies hygienically according to facility policy or home care guidelines (e.g., in a sealed plastic bag). <span class="rationale">Rationale: Proper emptying and disposal minimize odor, reduce the risk of spillage, and maintain hygiene and infection control.</span></li><li><strong>Manage Odor Effectively:</strong><span class="emoji">🌬️</span><span class="sub-point">Intervention:</span> Advise on and use odor-reducing strategies, such as ensuring a good pouch seal, using pouch deodorizers (liquid or tablet), and dietary advice regarding foods that may increase gas or odor (though individual tolerance varies). <span class="rationale">Rationale: Odor can be a major concern for patients with colostomies and can impact their social confidence. Effective odor management improves quality of life.</span></li></ol></div></div></div><div class="section"><h2><span class="ez-toc-section" id="SECTION_C_Long_Essay_Questions_60_Marks"></span>SECTION C: Long Essay Questions (60 Marks)<span class="ez-toc-section-end"></span></h2><div class="question"><span class="question-number">33(a)</span><div class="question-text"> Outline ten (10) important points a nurse should remember while caring for a patient with tracheostomy. (10 marks) </div><div class="answer-section essay-answer"><p><span class="emoji">⚕️</span>Caring for a patient with a tracheostomy at Nurses Revision Uganda requires specialized knowledge and meticulous attention to detail to maintain airway patency, prevent complications, and ensure patient comfort and safety. Here are ten important points nurses should remember:</p><ol><li><strong>Maintain a Patent Airway at All Times:</strong><span class="emoji">💨</span> This is the absolute priority. Ensure the tracheostomy tube is not kinked, dislodged, or obstructed by secretions. Regular assessment of breath sounds, respiratory effort, and oxygen saturation is crucial. <span class="rationale">Rationale: The tracheostomy is the patient's artificial airway. Any blockage can rapidly lead to hypoxia, respiratory arrest, and death.</span></li><li><strong>Perform Tracheostomy Suctioning As Clinically Indicated:</strong><span class="emoji">🌬️</span> Suction the tracheostomy tube only when necessary (e.g., audible secretions, signs of respiratory distress, desaturation) using sterile technique. Hyperoxygenate before and after suctioning (if indicated). Limit suction passes and duration to minimize trauma and hypoxia. <span class="rationale">Rationale: Suctioning clears secretions that the patient cannot expel, maintaining airway patency. However, it's an invasive procedure with potential risks, so it should be based on assessment, not routine.</span></li><li><strong>Provide Meticulous Tracheostomy Site and Tube Care:</strong><span class="emoji">🧼</span> Regularly clean the stoma site with sterile saline or other prescribed solution as per protocol. Assess for signs of infection (redness, swelling, discharge, odor). Clean or replace the inner cannula (if present) regularly according to policy to prevent obstruction from dried secretions. Change tracheostomy dressings and ties when soiled or damp, ensuring ties are secure but not too tight (allow one to two fingers underneath). <span class="rationale">Rationale: Proper site and tube care prevents infection, skin breakdown around the stoma, and tube obstruction, ensuring the integrity and functionality of the artificial airway.</span></li><li><strong>Ensure Adequate Humidification of Inspired Air:</strong><span class="emoji">💧</span> Since a tracheostomy bypasses the natural warming, filtering, and humidifying functions of the upper airway, inspired air must be humidified (e.g., via a heat and moisture exchanger - HME, nebulizer, or humidified oxygen). <span class="rationale">Rationale: Humidification prevents drying and thickening of respiratory secretions, reduces the risk of mucus plugging, maintains ciliary function, and prevents tracheal irritation or damage.</span></li><li><strong>Maintain Emergency Equipment at the Bedside:</strong><span class="emoji">🚨</span> Always have essential emergency equipment readily accessible at the patient's bedside. This includes: <ul class="sub-point"><li>A spare tracheostomy tube of the same size.</li><li>A spare tracheostomy tube one size smaller.</li><li>An obturator for the current tube size.</li><li>A tracheal dilator or spreader.</li><li>Suction catheters and suction source.</li><li>Ambu bag with mask and tracheostomy adapter.</li><li>Oxygen source and delivery devices.</li><li>Sterile gloves, saline, and dressings.</li></ul><span class="rationale">Rationale: In case of accidental decannulation (tube dislodgement) or acute obstruction, immediate access to this equipment is life-saving for re-establishing the airway.</span></li><li><strong>Monitor for and Prevent Complications:</strong><span class="emoji">⚠️</span> Be vigilant for potential complications such as tube obstruction, decannulation, bleeding, infection (stomal or respiratory), subcutaneous emphysema, tracheoesophageal fistula, or tracheal stenosis (long-term). <span class="rationale">Rationale: Early detection and prompt management of complications are crucial to prevent serious adverse outcomes. Regular assessment and adherence to best practices minimize these risks.</span></li><li><strong>Facilitate Effective Communication:</strong><span class="emoji">🗣️📝</span> Patients with tracheostomies (especially those with cuffed tubes or on ventilators) may be unable to speak. Provide alternative means of communication, such as a pen and paper, whiteboard, picture board, communication apps, or facilitate consultation for a speaking valve if appropriate and the patient is a candidate. <span class="rationale">Rationale: Inability to communicate can be extremely frustrating and isolating for the patient. Facilitating communication enhances their well-being, safety, and participation in care.</span></li><li><strong>Address Nutritional and Hydration Needs:</strong><span class="emoji">🍎💧</span> Assess the patient's ability to swallow. Some patients with tracheostomies may have dysphagia or be at risk of aspiration. Collaborate with the speech therapist and dietitian. Ensure adequate hydration to help keep secretions thin. <span class="rationale">Rationale: Safe and adequate nutrition and hydration are vital for recovery and overall health. Aspiration is a significant risk that needs careful management.</span></li><li><strong>Provide Psychological and Emotional Support:</strong><span class="emoji">❤️</span> Having a tracheostomy can be frightening and can significantly alter body image and self-esteem. Acknowledge the patient's fears and concerns. Provide reassurance, involve them in their care, and offer support. <span class="rationale">Rationale: Addressing the psychosocial impact of a tracheostomy is essential for the patient's overall well-being and adaptation to their altered airway.</span></li><li><strong>Educate the Patient and Family/Caregivers:</strong><span class="emoji">🧑‍🏫</span> Provide comprehensive education on all aspects of tracheostomy care, including suctioning, stoma care, emergency procedures (e.g., what to do if the tube comes out), signs of complications, and when to seek help. This is especially important if the patient is being discharged with a tracheostomy. <span class="rationale">Rationale: Education empowers the patient and their family to manage the tracheostomy safely and effectively at home, promoting independence and reducing anxiety and the risk of complications.</span></li></ol></div></div><div class="question"><span class="question-number">33(b)</span><div class="question-text"> Describe ten (10) nursing responsibilities to a patient undergoing abdominal paracentesis. (10 marks) </div><div class="answer-section essay-answer"><p><span class="emoji">💧🧑‍⚕️</span>Abdominal paracentesis is an invasive procedure to remove ascitic fluid from the peritoneal cavity for diagnostic or therapeutic purposes. Nurses at Nurses Revision Uganda have crucial responsibilities before, during, and after the procedure to ensure patient safety, comfort, and optimal outcomes.</p><p><strong>Before the Procedure:</strong></p><ol><li><strong>Verify Informed Consent and Patient Understanding:</strong><span class="emoji">✅🗣️</span><span class="sub-point">Responsibility:</span> Ensure that a valid informed consent form has been signed by the patient (or legal guardian). Reinforce the explanation of the procedure, its purpose, potential benefits, risks, and alternatives. Answer any questions the patient may have. <span class="rationale">Rationale: Upholds patient autonomy and legal requirements. Ensures the patient is fully aware of what to expect and has agreed to the procedure, which can reduce anxiety.</span></li><li><strong>Assess Baseline Vital Signs and Abdominal Girth:</strong><span class="emoji">🩺📏</span><span class="sub-point">Responsibility:</span> Obtain and record baseline vital signs (temperature, pulse, respirations, blood pressure, SpO2) and measure the patient's abdominal girth at the level of the umbilicus (mark the site for consistency). Also, assess baseline weight if indicated. <span class="rationale">Rationale: Provides a baseline for comparison during and after the procedure to detect any adverse changes (e.g., hypotension if a large volume of fluid is removed). Abdominal girth and weight help quantify the amount of ascites and monitor the effectiveness of therapeutic paracentesis.</span></li><li><strong>Instruct and Assist the Patient to Empty Their Bladder:</strong><span class="emoji">🚽</span><span class="sub-point">Responsibility:</span> Instruct the patient to void (empty their bladder) completely just before the procedure. If the patient is unable to void, notify the physician as catheterization may be considered. <span class="rationale">Rationale: An empty bladder reduces its size and moves it away from the typical needle insertion site in the lower abdomen, significantly minimizing the risk of accidental bladder perforation during the paracentesis.</span></li><li><strong>Gather and Prepare Necessary Equipment and Supplies:</strong><span class="emoji">🛠️</span><span class="sub-point">Responsibility:</span> Assemble all required sterile equipment, including the paracentesis tray (containing items like local anesthetic, needles, syringes, drapes, antiseptic solution, trocar/catheter), sterile gloves, collection containers/bottles (may need to be vacuum-sealed), laboratory specimen tubes (if diagnostic samples are needed), and a dressing for the puncture site. <span class="rationale">Rationale: Ensures all necessary items are readily available, promoting efficiency and maintaining sterility during the procedure, thereby reducing the risk of delays or infection.</span></li></ol><p><strong>During the Procedure:</strong></p><ol start="5"><li><strong>Position the Patient Appropriately and Provide Comfort:</strong><span class="emoji">🛌🧘</span><span class="sub-point">Responsibility:</span> Assist the patient into the correct position, typically sitting upright in bed (High Fowler's) or on the side of the bed leaning over an overbed table, with feet supported. Ensure patient comfort and provide reassurance. <span class="rationale">Rationale: An upright position allows ascitic fluid to pool in the lower abdomen by gravity, facilitating easier needle insertion and fluid drainage. It also allows the bowel to float posteriorly, away from the anterior puncture site. Comfort measures help reduce patient anxiety.</span></li><li><strong>Assist the Physician and Maintain Aseptic Technique:</strong><span class="emoji">🧑‍⚕️🧤</span><span class="sub-point">Responsibility:</span> Assist the physician as needed during the procedure (e.g., by providing sterile supplies, labeling specimen containers). Strictly maintain aseptic technique throughout to prevent introducing infection into the peritoneal cavity. <span class="rationale">Rationale: Asepsis is crucial to prevent peritonitis, a serious complication. Teamwork between nurse and physician ensures the procedure is performed smoothly and safely.</span></li><li><strong>Monitor Patient's Vital Signs and Tolerance of the Procedure:</strong><span class="emoji">💓⚠️</span><span class="sub-point">Responsibility:</span> Continuously monitor the patient's vital signs (especially blood pressure and heart rate), level of consciousness, skin color, and any complaints of pain, dizziness, shortness of breath, or nausea during fluid removal. <span class="rationale">Rationale: Rapid removal of large volumes of ascitic fluid can lead to significant fluid shifts and complications such as hypotension, vasovagal reaction, or electrolyte imbalances. Close monitoring allows for early detection and intervention if adverse reactions occur.</span></li></ol><p><strong>After the Procedure:</strong></p><ol start="8"><li><strong>Apply a Sterile Dressing and Monitor the Puncture Site:</strong><span class="emoji">🩹</span><span class="sub-point">Responsibility:</span> After the needle/catheter is removed, apply firm pressure to the puncture site briefly (if needed) and then apply a sterile dressing. Regularly inspect the site for any leakage of ascitic fluid, bleeding, or signs of infection. <span class="rationale">Rationale: The dressing protects the site from infection. Monitoring for leakage is important as persistent leakage can occur and may require further management (e.g., a pressure dressing, or rarely, a suture).</span></li><li><strong>Monitor Post-Procedure Vital Signs, Abdominal Girth, and Weight:</strong><span class="emoji">📉⚖️</span><span class="sub-point">Responsibility:</span> Continue to monitor vital signs at specified intervals (e.g., every 15 mins for an hour, then less frequently if stable). Re-measure abdominal girth and weight (if done pre-procedure) to assess the amount of fluid removed and the patient's response. <span class="rationale">Rationale: Post-procedure monitoring helps detect delayed complications like hypotension, hypovolemia (if large volumes removed without albumin replacement in some cases), or re-accumulation of ascites.</span></li><li><strong>Document the Procedure and Patient's Response:</strong><span class="emoji">✍️</span><span class="sub-point">Responsibility:</span> Accurately document all aspects of the procedure, including pre-procedure preparations, patient tolerance, amount and characteristics (color, clarity) of fluid drained, any specimens sent to the lab, vital signs, post-procedure assessments, and any interventions performed or complications noted. <span class="rationale">Rationale: Comprehensive documentation is essential for legal purposes, communication among the healthcare team, continuity of care, and for evaluating the patient's progress and response to the therapeutic intervention.</span></li><li><strong>Educate the Patient on Post-Procedure Care and Signs to Report:</strong><span class="emoji">🗣️🆘</span><span class="sub-point">Responsibility:</span> Instruct the patient on care of the puncture site, activity restrictions (if any), and signs and symptoms of potential complications to report to the healthcare provider after discharge (e.g., fever, increasing abdominal pain or tenderness, redness or drainage from the site, dizziness, rapid re-accumulation of fluid). <span class="rationale">Rationale: Patient education empowers them to participate in their own care, recognize early warning signs of complications, and seek timely medical attention if needed.</span></li></ol></div></div><div class="question"><span class="question-number">34(a)</span><div class="question-text"> Outline the ten (10) general principles for bandaging. (10 marks) </div><div class="answer-section essay-answer"><p><span class="emoji">🩹</span>Bandaging is a common nursing procedure at Nurses Revision Uganda used for various purposes such as supporting an injured part, immobilizing a joint, securing a dressing, applying pressure to control bleeding, or promoting venous return. Adherence to general principles is crucial for effectiveness and patient safety.</p><ol><li><strong>Ensure Proper Patient Positioning and Comfort:</strong><span class="emoji">🧘</span> Position the patient comfortably and ensure the body part to be bandaged is well-supported and in the desired anatomical alignment (e.g., a joint in a functional position or position of rest) before starting. <span class="rationale">Rationale: Proper positioning makes the bandaging process easier for the nurse, more comfortable for the patient, and ensures the bandage is applied to maintain the desired alignment or function once completed.</span></li><li><strong>Select the Appropriate Type and Size of Bandage:</strong><span class="emoji">📏</span> Choose a bandage material (e.g., gauze, elastic, crepe, adhesive) and width that is appropriate for the size of the body part being bandaged and the purpose of the bandage. <span class="rationale">Rationale: Using the correct type and size ensures the bandage can effectively achieve its purpose (e.g., a wider bandage for a larger limb, an elastic bandage for compression). An inappropriately sized bandage can be ineffective or cause constriction.</span></li><li><strong>Maintain Cleanliness/Asepsis as Appropriate:</strong><span class="emoji">🧼</span> Wash hands before starting. If bandaging an open wound, use aseptic technique and sterile materials where indicated.
